speaker: interviewer
text: 17th, 1996, interviewee General Felix Sparks. Interviewer Toni Katsh Binstock, in Lakewood, Colorado, United States of America. And it's being done in English. At the end of the last tape, General, you said you didn't think there was anything more. Is there anything else that you wanted to add or that I've forgotten to ask you about?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I can't think of anything. There is a lady here-- I think she's still alive. I can't think of her name offhand, but I have met with her a couple of times. It was out, I believe, in Englewood or Littleton-- who was a reporter at the war crimes trial.

speaker: interviewer
text: Vivien Spitz, I believe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Vivien, that's her name. And she has a wealth of information, because she has-- a lot of the exhibits, she kept. She went through that whole trial. You know, that trial-- people don't realize, it but those trials lasted for five years. That trial lasted for five years. And it went through different groups-- Nazi doctors. I have a book here about the Nazi doctors and the-- and the various groups, which started with a head group. Well they didn't get Goebbels. He committed suicide. But the fat boy-- Hermann Goering-- was tried. That group-- the leadership was tried first. And after, they broke down into a number of different categories, like the Nazi doctors, the professors. One of the interesting things about the German mentality-- this Dr. Rascher-- who was the guy who conducted, I think, the high altitude and the freezing experiments of Dachau-- his wife, apparently, was not able to bear children. Well, Hitler, of course-- you got a medal in Germany if you had so many kids-- boys, not girls. So many boys, you got a bronze medal, so many more, a silver medal, and a gold medal-- those were given out for the wives. Apparently Dr. Rascher's wife was not able to conceive. So she got a child from some source-- a baby. He could have gotten it out of a concentration camp, because women had-- if they weren't executed, probably some women had babies in the concentration camp. Anyway, she passed it off as her own. And she named Heinrich Himmler as the godfather. And Himmler visited him, because he was carrying on his experiments at Dachau. I have some of her letters that she had written to him, or he had written to her. And in one letter, she expresses her great appreciation for his visit to visit his godchild. Well, somehow or other, somebody discovered that she didn't really have that baby. So that made old Himmler mad to no end.

speaker: interviewer
text: So her husband Dr. Rascher was executed at Dachau. He was hung. And she was executed. That's how far they carried the ideological thing. So he would have been executed, anyway, as a result of the war trial.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dr. Schilling, who was the one that constructed the experiments on various diseases, like malaria and so, he was apprehended after the war. And he was executed. He was one of the German doctors who was executed. But Rascher escaped the execution-- American execution. The Germans executed him because of the false identity. That's how far they carried the thing. You know, it's incredible.

speaker: interviewer
text: One thing I did forget to ask is how you were-- what kind of metals that you were given during the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, high was wounded twice, so I got two Purple Hearts. I got two Silver Stars and Meritorious Service Medal, Legion of Merit. From the French government, I got the French Croix de Guerre. I don't know. Mostly I never paid much attention to medals. I don't even know where they are. I got them around here somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: General Sparks, it has been an honor to interview you. And thank you so much for participating in the Shoah project.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know what you're going to do with all this. I did meet old Steven Spielberg at the governor's mansion here a year or so ago. He was a very ordinary looking and talking guy, you know, when you just meet him.

speaker: interviewer
text: General Sparks, will you introduce your wife to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is my wife, Mary-- my wife of some 55 years and the mother of our four children. We met while we were students at the University of Arizona, when we were both freshmen. She graduated from the University of Arizona. We were married while she-- at the end of her junior year, and I was in the Army at that time. It was in June of 1941. Little did we realize that about six months later or eight months later Pearl Harbor would come along and change our lives rather drastically. So we didn't see much of each other in our first years of marriage. I was overseas for 2 and 1/2 years. And she had a baby about the time that I was departing for Africa. And I didn't see the baby-- a little boy-- until he was about 2 and 1/2 years old. But it's been a long, hectic 55 years, but very rewarding

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Sparks, do you have anything you'd like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I will say that over all these years, it's been anything but dull. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Has his-- did his war experiences and what he's participated in since then-- how did that affect your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it affected it a lot, you know. Like, he's had all kinds of experiences and parts to his life that I was included in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you very, very much both of you for appearing today. What is this picture, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a picture taken at Fort Kamehameha, Hawaii. I was a corporal at that time and a member of an artillery regiment which was-- was supposed to be protecting Pearl Harbor. We were actually at the mouth of Pearl Harbor, and we manned the big guns. We were still anticipating invasion by land in those days. We did not anticipate the invasion by air that actually occurred. My old regiment never fired a shot when the Japanese hit Pearl Harbor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And what is this picture, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a picture taken in Washington, DC, in about 1939. At that time, the United States used to be divided into four-- or nine core areas. And annually, an award was given to the outstanding military cadet in each of the core areas-- the nine core areas. This was a General Pershing award. And the medal is being presented to the nine of us by General George Marshall, who at that time was the Army Chief of Staff.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which one are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm in the back row there, in the middle in the back row-- the tallest one you can see in the back row. We were awarded a three-day trip to Washington, DC, all expenses paid. And we were entertained very royally while we're here in Washington, DC.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is General Marshall in that picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: General Marshall is there. He's the one presenting the medal. Oh, he's over here on the end. That's General Marshall right there, pinning on-- he pinned on the medal on each one of us, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's a wedding picture of my wife and I, taken on June 17th, 1941, at Tucson, Arizona. I was a-- I had been called back to the service at that time. I was a second lieutenant. She was a student at the University of Arizona, and her family lived in Tucson, Arizona, where we were married.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a picture taken on the Anzio beachhead. This is Colonel Krieger on the left and me on the right. He was the battalion commander at that time, and I was the battalion executive officer. And subsequently, both he and I were battalion commanders all through the French and German campaign. He was a very, very fine officer. Unfortunately, he passed away earlier this year.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in 1961, when I was called back to active duty as the commander of an artillery group for artillery battalions that were scheduled to take place in the invasion of Cuba if it was going-- if it occurred. I was designated as a strike commander. And this was at Fort Sill, Oklahoma.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That picture was taken at my retirement ceremony at Fort Carson in 1977. And at that time, I was presented with the Legion of Merit award. And on that day, I retired from the Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your rank?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a Brigadier General at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That book, 'Sparks,' was written by Emajean Buechner of New Orleans, Louisiana. Her husband was my battalion surgeon, medical doctor, in France and Germany. And what she did was take all of my memoirs that I had written about the various battles, and it covers my whole five-year period as a member of the 157th Infantry Regiment.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the pictures that we looked at before.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the pictures that you saw before were all in that book. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'll have to look at this when I can see it better. In

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was-- that picture was taken on the occasion of our 50th wedding anniversary here in Lakewood about five years ago, now. And the picture was presented to us by our children, who gave us this little-- little private party just for our family. And in the back row, starting over on the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, on the left, would be our oldest son. He was the one who was born, and I didn't see him till he was 2 and 1/2 years old. Next to him is his son. His name is Kirk. Next to him is his son, Spencer, who is now in the United States Army. He'll be home pretty quick. And the next one is his other son, Blair, named after his grandmother. My wife's maiden name was Blair. He served four years in the United States Air Force. And the other is one of our twins-- twin grandsons, Steve, The daughter-- children of our daughter Kim is in the next row. Next to him is the other grandson, the one who was murdered three years ago-- Lee. And then starting on the extreme left-- right over there is our youngest son, Scott. Next to him is Kim, the mother of the two twin boys. And next to her is one of our granddaughters, the daughter of our-- Natalie is her name. And next to her is her mother. Jean Ann is our oldest daughter. And then going down the line here, that's another-- her other daughter, Tammy, Tamara. She has those two daughters. And then next to her is a Korean wife of our son, Kirk. He met her while he was stationed in Korea. They were married there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is [KOREAN]. And then, of course, my wife and I-- the older people there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very nice, thank you. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Those pictures were taken by a soldier, an artilleryman, who was attached to my battalion. The man's name was Ed [? Gorakh. ?] He was quite an ardent photographer. He's one of the-- since being an artillery, he could carry a camera around. Most of the infantrymen could not. But he took a great many pictures while we were overseas. And he took these shortly after the liberation of Dachau. And he blew those up for me and sent me those pictures. The top row is all portrayed as the victims and the railway cars that were at the edge of Dachau, the concentration camp, when we first went in. See the rather young lady in that middle picture was dead in one of those railway cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: The second row-- there are pictures taken at the crematorium. And one of the pictures on the end is where they had hauled the bodies, stripped of clothing, and they were stacked there, ready to be put into the furnaces. The-- those are prisoners, camp inmates, who posed for these pictures and showed us how it was done. But they were the ones who were compelled to handle all the bodies and put them into the crematorium, where they were then reduced to ashes. There were four of those furnaces. They were coal-fired furnaces.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The bottom row, the one the extreme right there, is another picture of the coal-fired furnaces where they cremated the bodies.

speaker: interviewer
text: The left or the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, on the end, it shows the furnaces. The middle picture shows an area where they-- they stripped the corpses of all the clothing, took out any silver or gold fillings that they had in their teeth, then they hung those clothes up in a rack there, and they issued them to the other inmates. They never wasted any clothing. But they stripped all the dead of all clothing before they cremated them. Then they had stacks of them hanging up there in that building. The end picture on the end here is a picture of the confinement area, or the prison area, showing the prison barracks, the barbed wire fence, and then a path running right along the barbed wire fence, and then-- then the moat, a moat filled with water, which surrounded the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was one of the first sites I saw as I approached the confinement area, or the prison area. Those were human bodies, been stripped of clothing. They were inside the prison area. Now, those were getting-- being prepared for hauling to the crematorium for cremation. But the Germans left before they picked those up and cremated them. But there were about 200 dead, nude bodies in that location, right inside the prison area.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's another picture of much the same-- of the same scene. Those were bodies of people who had died inside the prison. And they were laid out, ready to be hauled out to the crematorium, but they never got around to doing that before we arrived. That was a picture taken showing the bodies inside one of the railway cars that we found at the entrance to the camp. In the 39 railway cars, there were about 2,000 dead people who died en route from Buchenwald to Dachau. That picture was taken-- that was after the cleanup of the bodies started. We started collecting bodies from various locations, many of them still had clothing on. There were still-- there were quite a number who died inside the prison area who were still clothed. And this was part of the cleanup operation where we're carrying them and piling them up for burial. That's a picture of German guards who were killed by one of my men with a machine gun. There were about 11 bodies in that pile. He killed them right there at that location up against a wall, a cement wall. That's taken next to the crematorium. There was a small cement building where they piled the nude bodies in that small room in preparation for putting them through the crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did not think this was used as a gas chamber, is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was a gas chamber there, but it was never used for-- that was not the gas chamber. It was never used for extermination. That was a room where they-- a collecting point where they collected the bodies and that was next to the furnaces. This is-- he always said when he got to it, he was going to piss in the Rhine River. Soon as we got to the Rhine, and that's what he's doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. This was given to me by a friend from the Third Army Headquarters some years ago. I'd forgotten I had it for years, and I found it not long ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's the one on the left, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left-- on the extreme left--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your left.

speaker: interviewee
text: --is General Patton.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did he say he was going to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he said he was going to piss in the Rhine River, and that's what he was doing. He's got it out and going at it there.
